petitioner_background: The petitioner is an entrepreneur in the hotel and motel industry, specifically operating as a Super 8 franchisee in small-town Texas communities (notably Kingsville). He incorporated his own hotel business in 2002 and seeks classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in business under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). His Form I-140 listed his occupation as 'Entrepreneur' and job title as 'Renowned Entrepreneur ... in the hotel and motel industry.' The petition was filed on June 5, 2009 through the Texas Service Center.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Letters stating that the petitioner is a member of the Asian American Hotel Owners Association (and similar hotel/motel owner associations). No bylaws, rules of admission, or other documentation of the organizations' membership requirements were submitted.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that to meet this criterion, the petitioner must show that the association requires outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission, as judged by recognized national or international experts. Membership based on employment, activity, education, experience, test scores, GPA, colleague recommendations, or payment of dues does not qualify. The record did not include evidence of the membership requirements (such as bylaws or rules of admission) showing outstanding achievements were required. Further, on appeal the petitioner did not contest the director's findings, so the AAO considered the issue abandoned under Sepulveda and Hristov.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An article entitled [title redacted] in the June 2009 issue of the Wrangler, 'A publication of the [organization redacted]'; and a June 28, 2009 article in the Kingsville Journal entitled '[petitioner] unveil refurbished [hotel].'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the Wrangler article failed because the author was not identified, as required by the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The Kingsville Journal article was dated June 28, 2009, which post-dated the June 5, 2009 filing date; under Matter of Katigbak and Matter of Izummi, eligibility must be established at the time of filing and facts arising after filing cannot be considered. Additionally, there was no circulation evidence or other documentation showing that the Wrangler or the Kingsville Journal qualify as major trade publications or major media. The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on appeal, so the issue was considered abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner's academic records; evidence of financial transactions; documentation of his incorporation of his hotel company including financial statements, corporate income tax returns for 2004-2008, brochures for his motel, photographs of the motel, a commercial [redacted], and miscellaneous tax documents; a franchise agreement between his company and Super 8; financial statements, a 2008 corporate income tax return; certificates of filing, articles of organization, regulations, corporate income tax returns, financial statements, bank documentation relating to other local hotel properties; and letters of support from the Executive Director of the [Kingsville] Chamber of Commerce, the Executive Director of a Convention & Visitors Bureau, the President & CEO of [an entity], a representative of the Young Performers Competitions of the Music [organization], the Mayor of Kingsville, and the Director of Operations and Support for the Super 8 Motels, Inc. franchise. Letters praised the petitioner's entrepreneurial talents and discussed his projects including the 'micro-investment' strategy allowing investors to pool funds.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence showing the submitted documentation constituted original business-related contributions of major significance in the hotel or motel industry. The AAO stated it is not enough to be talented and have others attest to talent; an alien must have demonstrably impacted his field. Vague, solicited letters from local colleagues that do not specifically identify original contributions or provide specific examples of how those contributions influenced the field are insufficient (citing Kazarian, 580 F.3d 1030, 1036). The references did not explain how the petitioner's contributions were original or provide specific examples of major significance in the field. Citing Silverman v. Eastrich, the AAO noted the phrase 'major significance' is not superfluous. The record did not indicate the extent of the petitioner's influence on trends in the hotel and motel industry beyond the local area, nor show that the field had significantly changed as a result of his work. The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on appeal, so the issue was considered abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner's resume; an autobiographical statement; a business card identifying him as [title redacted]; a business card identifying him as a Super 8 franchisee; and letters of support from the Kingsville Chamber of Commerce Executive Director, a Convention & Visitors Bureau Executive Director, the President & CEO of [an entity], the Young Performers Competitions representative, the Mayor of Kingsville, and the Director of Operations and Support for Super 8 Motels, Inc. Petitioner also asserted he performed in a leading/critical role as Director, President, and owner of his own hotel company (incorporated 2002), and referenced a role as 'Group Leader' of an aviation-related entity.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted Form I-140 listed his occupation as 'Entrepreneur' in the hotel and motel industry, and the statute requires the petitioner to continue work in his area of expertise. Because there was no evidence he intended to continue in the aviation industry, his role as Group Leader in that entity did not constitute evidence of extraordinary ability in the hotel and motel industry. The AAO found not every employee or franchise general manager who performs competently meets this criterion. The Super 8 Director's letter described the petitioner as 'a valued member' and 'highly successful franchisee,' but provided no specific financial data demonstrating the profitability of petitioner's Super 8 franchise locations in relation to the company's other franchisees. Citing 1756, Inc. v. Attorney General, USCIS need not accept conclusory assertions. The evidence did not demonstrate how his positions distinguished him from other managers or top executives; there was no organizational chart showing his position within company hierarchy. There was no documentary evidence that Super 8 or the other companies had earned a distinguished reputation. For his own company, there was no documentary evidence it earned a distinguished reputation since its 2002 incorporation. Citing Matter of Soffici, going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient. The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on appeal, so the issue was considered abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner's and his spouse's unsigned U.S. Individual Income Tax Returns for 2007 and 2008 reflecting total income [amounts redacted]. In response to the RFE, petitioner submitted Foreign Labor Certification Data Center 'Online Wage Library' wage information for 'Chief Executives' indicating the Level 4 (fully competent) 'prevailing wage' in the [local] metropolitan area.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the tax returns were unsigned, did not identify an Employer Identification Number in Schedule C, did not include Form W-2 Wage and Tax Statements, and did not distinguish between income attributable to the petitioner versus his spouse. The AAO held the petitioner did not establish the Chief Executive wage data was comparable to those performing similar work in the hotel and motel industry. Reliance on local 'prevailing' wages is not an appropriate basis for comparison in demonstrating a high salary in relation to others in the field. Citing Matter of Price (professional golfer vs. other PGA Tour golfers), Grimson v. INS (NHL enforcer vs. other NHL enforcers), and Muni v. INS (NHL defenseman vs. other NHL defensemen), comparisons must be within the specific field. The evidence did not establish the petitioner's actual salary, let alone that it was high in relation to others in the field. The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on appeal, so the issue was considered abandoned.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, concurring with the director that the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award or to meet at least three of the ten regulatory categories of evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). In the final merits determination, the AAO found that even considering the evidence in the aggregate, the petitioner did not distinguish himself as one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of his field. The AAO rejected counsel's 'comparable evidence' argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) because the petitioner failed to show the ten categories did not readily apply to his occupation — comparable evidence is not allowed simply because a petitioner cannot meet three categories. The AAO emphasized the petitioner's recognition was limited to his local Kingsville community and direct business acquaintances, which does not satisfy the 'sustained national or international acclaim' standard. It also declined to rely on the petitioner-prepared 'Investment Summary' as self-promotional material (citing Braga v. Poulos), and noted that employment creation aliens have a separate visa classification under INA § 203(b)(5).
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025, aff'd 345 F.3d 683
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
1756, Inc. v. The Attorney General of the United States, 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
Grimson v. INS, 934 F. Supp. 965 (N.D. Ill. 1996)
Muni v. INS, 891 F. Supp. 440 (N.D. Ill. 1995)
Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
Avyr Associates, Inc. v. Meissner, 1997 WL 188942 (S.D.N.Y.)
Braga v. Poulos, No. CV 06 5105 SJO (C.D. CA July 6, 2007), aff'd 317 Fed. Appx. 680 (9th Cir. 2009)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted several procedural deficiencies: (1) the petitioner submitted unsigned tax returns that lacked an Employer Identification Number on Schedule C and did not include Form W-2s; (2) one submitted article (Kingsville Journal, June 28, 2009) post-dated the June 5, 2009 filing date and could not be considered under Matter of Katigbak and Matter of Izummi, which require eligibility to be established at the time of filing; (3) the Wrangler article failed to identify its author, violating the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii); (4) the 'Investment Summary' document was prepared by the petitioner himself and contained self-estimated employee numbers, which the AAO treated as self-promotional and unreliable under Braga v. Poulos; and (5) the petitioner failed to contest the director's findings on multiple criteria on appeal, causing those issues to be deemed abandoned under Sepulveda and Hristov. No fraud or willful misrepresentation findings were made.
